research_series.series_name: Cerebral Perfusion, CO2, Oxygenation, Orthostatic Regulation, Dizziness, Fatigue, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Orthostatic challenge can reduce cerebral blood flow and produce dizziness, fatigue, brain fog, and cognitive impairment even without systemic hypotension

mediator.name: reduced_cerebral_blood_flow_or_oxygenation
mediator.pathway: upright posture or orthostatic stress reduces cerebral perfusion/oxygenation despite sometimes normal systemic BP
mediator.mechanism: autonomic dysregulation, impaired autoregulation, hypocapnia-mediated vasoconstriction, hypovolemia, or microvascular dysfunction

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: If symptoms worsen upright and dynamic testing shows reduced cerebral blood flow/oxygenation, infer orthostatic cerebral hypoperfusion as a strong contributor to dizziness/brain fog/fatigue.

hypotheses.reason: Tilt/TCD/NIRS studies repeatedly show CBF/oxygenation reductions linked to symptoms across POTS, ME/CFS, long COVID and OH.

evidence.summary: Dynamic orthostatic/respiratory challenges can reduce cerebral perfusion or oxygenation and are linked to dizziness, fatigue, brain fog and cognitive impairment, often missed by seated BP/HR alone.

key_sources.title: Cerebral blood flow is reduced in ME/CFS during head-up tilt testing even in the absence of hypotension or tachycardia
key_sources.relevant_finding: CBF reduction during tilt in ME/CFS even without hypotension/tachycardia.

key_sources.title: Orthostatic intolerance with tachycardia and hypocapnic cerebral hypoperfusion represent a spectrum
key_sources.relevant_finding: ETCO2 drop predicts CBFv decline and symptoms independent of HR/BP.

limitations: Not all OH cases lead to cognitive decline
Dynamic measures are not always paired with simultaneous cognitive testing

patterns.pattern_name: Hypocapnia during upright posture reduces cerebral blood flow via vasoconstriction and can mimic POTS symptoms without tachycardia

mediator.name: hypocapnia_mediated_cerebral_vasoconstriction
mediator.mechanism: ETCO2 drop reduces CBFv; reported estimate about 3% CBF reduction per 1 mmHg ETCO2 decrease

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Normal SpO2 and normal BP do not rule out respiratory/CO2-mediated cerebral hypoperfusion; check ETCO2/capnography or breathing pattern when dizziness/brain fog occurs upright.

hypotheses.reason: ETCO2 reduction predicts CBFv decline and symptoms during orthostasis.

evidence.summary: Hypocapnia is a strong mechanism linking orthostatic or hyperventilation states to reduced cerebral perfusion and symptoms.

limitations: Not all long COVID cerebral hypoperfusion is hypocapnia-mediated
Fast breathing does not always imply hypocapnia

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Normal seated blood pressure excludes orthostatic cerebral hypoperfusion.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: CBF/oxygenation abnormalities can occur during tilt even without hypotension or tachycardia.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: All orthostatic hypotension causes cognitive decline.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Some older-adult cohorts find no cross-sectional association; susceptibility and dynamic testing matter.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Normal SpO2 excludes respiratory contribution to brain fog.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Hypocapnia/ETCO2 abnormalities can reduce CBF without hypoxemia.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Reduced cerebral blood flow/oxygenation during upright posture or orthostatic challenge.
Reduced cerebral perfusion due to hypocapnia-mediated cerebral vasoconstriction.

research_gaps: Need simultaneous hemodynamic and objective cognitive testing during controlled challenge.
Need interventions showing that improving upright CBF improves brain fog/fatigue.
Need ambulatory measures of cerebral oxygenation/perfusion linked to daily symptoms.

most_supported_patterns: Orthostatic/respiratory challenges reduce cerebral perfusion/oxygenation and can cause dizziness/fatigue/brain fog/cognitive impairment.
Hypocapnia-mediated vasoconstriction is a key mechanism of CBF reduction.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Symptoms during upright posture plus reduced CBF/oxygenation strongly support cerebral hypoperfusion branch.
Normal BP/SpO2 does not rule out orthostatic/CO2 mechanisms.
recommended_next_queries: sickness behavior neuroimmune signaling fatigue motivation cognition
metabolic flexibility mitochondrial energy fatigue cognition
summary.overall_assessment: Strong deep-chain series. It integrates orthostatic, respiratory, volume and autonomic branches into a mechanistic cerebral perfusion pathway for dizziness, fatigue and brain fog.

works_cited.title: Consensus review PDF: Orthostatic, Respiratory, and Volume-Mediated Changes in Cerebral Perfusion
